background.occupation: Marketing Analytics Manager at a mid-sized tech firm
background.social_class: Middle Class (rents a 1-bedroom apartment in a gentrifying neighborhood, drives a 5-year-old sedan)
background.key_experience: In 2020, her company implemented mandatory productivity-tracking software that monitored keystrokes and screen activity. She initially defended it as 'necessary for remote work efficiency,' but after being flagged for 'low activity' during a migraine episode (which she'd reported), she received a formal warning. This made her deeply distrustful of workplace surveillance tech but also paradoxically more accepting of it in consumer contexts, believing 'if it's happening anyway, might as well get the benefits.'

dominant_traits: Very Low Openness to Experience (resists new ideas, prefers routine)
Moderate Conscientiousness (organized but only when externally pressured)

cognition.core_value: Conformity to perceived majority opinion—'If everyone's doing it, there must be a good reason, and I don't want to be left behind.'
cognition.bias: Bandwagon Effect, triggered by seeing trending hashtags or viral posts about tech products/policies. Example: She'll adopt strong pro- or anti-AI stances based on which side has more visible social media momentum that week, then retroactively justify it with cherry-picked articles.

current_state.recent_memory: Yesterday, her LinkedIn feed was flooded with posts from colleagues praising a new AI meeting-summarizer tool. She spent 20 minutes crafting a comment like 'Game-changer for productivity! 🚀'—then immediately felt a pang of anxiety that she might be expected to use it.
current_state.emotion: Cynically excited—a jittery mix of FOMO-driven enthusiasm about tech trends and underlying resentment that she's constantly adapting to tools she didn't choose.
